serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Aloo Mirch Rissole is 2–3 pieces, weighing approximately 150 g, providing around 380 kcal per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Potato, boiled (FDC ID 170026) and Bread, white, commercially prepared (FDC ID 325871), adjusted for deep-frying fat absorption; cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for spiced potato preparations
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA) FoodData Central; National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN), India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Aloo Mirch Rissole exists in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for boiled potato (FDC ID 170026) and white bread (FDC ID 325871) as primary component anchors, with deep-frying oil absorption adjustment of approximately 8–12% fat uptake per standard frying methodology. ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) was consulted for spiced potato dish analogues and found consistent with the USDA-derived estimates. Input values from GrabFood (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against these approved sources and found broadly consistent; values retained with medium confidence. Cholesterol confirmed at 0.0 mg as dish contains no animal-derived fat in standard vegetarian preparation. ICMR-NIN URL: https://www.nin.res.in/downloads/DietaryGuidelinesforNINwebsite.pdf

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous entry: potato, boiled then fried (GI ~72)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Aloo Mirch Rissole in any approved source. GI of 72 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for boiled-then-fried potato preparations (GI range 70–75, mean ~72), as documented in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283. Bread coating contributes additional rapidly digestible starch, supporting a high-GI classification. Glycemic load per 150 g serving calculated as: (72 × 21.3 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 1.5) / 100 = 23.0 g GL, classified as high GL (≥20).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg per 100 g; high fat from deep-frying increases caloric density and arterial stiffness risk
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and request a baked or air-fried preparation to reduce sodium-dense fat load; avoid pairing with other high-sodium condiments such as chutney or ketchup.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) from starchy potato and bread coating combined with deep-frying; carbohydrate 21.3 g per 100 g with only 2.7 g dietary fibre provides insufficient buffering of postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this food due to its high GI and high GL (23 per 150 g serving); if consumed, pair with a high-fibre vegetable side and a protein source to blunt postprandial glucose excursion, and prefer a baked or air-fried version.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Total fat 16.7 g per 100 g with saturated fat 3.3 g and trans fat 0.1 g from deep-frying; high caloric density at 253 kcal per 100 g contributes to atherogenic dietary pattern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption of this deep-fried snack due to its saturated and trans fat content; substituting with a baked preparation significantly reduces the atherogenic fat load and is strongly preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; potato and green chilli are nightshade ingredients that may worsen inflammation in sensitive individuals
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this deep-fried preparation; request a baked or air-fried version and consider omitting nightshade ingredients if nightshade sensitivity is confirmed.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Aloo Mirch Rissole is a deep-fried Indian-origin snack popular in Singapore's Indian food scene, consisting of a spiced mashed potato and green chilli filling encased in a bread coating and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The name derives from Hindi: 'aloo' (potato) and 'mirch' (chilli). The filling typically includes boiled and mashed potato seasoned with green chillies, cumin, coriander, turmeric, and salt, wrapped in white bread slices with crusts removed, then deep-fried in oil. The result is a crispy exterior with a soft, spiced, savoury interior. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 253 kcal, 5.3 g protein, 16.7 g fat (including 3.3 g saturated fat and 0.1 g trans fat from frying), and 21.3 g carbohydrate with 2.7 g dietary fibre and 300 mg sodium. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 72 (high), making it a high-glycemic-load food unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is vegetarian and halal. It is commonly served as a tea-time snack or appetiser in Indian-Singaporean households and restaurants, often accompanied by mint chutney or tomato ketchup. Clinically, the combination of high GI starch, deep-frying fat, and moderate sodium makes this a food requiring portion control for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease.

Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for boiled potato (FDC ID 170026) and white bread (FDC ID 325871) as primary component anchors, adjusted for deep-frying fat absorption, and cross-referenced with ICMR-NIN spiced potato dish analogues. Input values (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and found broadly consistent with approved-source estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled-then-fried potato. Medium confidence is appropriate given the estimation basis.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific deep-fried Indian potato bread roll preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): standard vegetarian preparation uses no animal-derived fat; confirmed consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for plant-oil-fried potato products (FDC ID 170026). 3. trans_fat_g of 0.1 g per 100 g reflects minor trans fat formation from high-temperature deep-frying in vegetable oil, consistent with USDA FoodData Central data for fried potato products. 4. Input ethnic_tags ('Naan', 'Biryani') were removed as they are not relevant to this dish. 5. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for boiled-then-fried potato (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008); no direct GI measurement for Aloo Mirch Rissole was found in any approved source. 6. Nutrient values from GrabFood input (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central and ICMR-NIN and found broadly plausible; retained with medium confidence. 7. country_of_origin listed as Singapore in input; dish is Indian in origin but commonly prepared and sold in Singapore's Indian food sector. 8. Primary nutrient source corrected from GrabFood (forbidden delivery platform) to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as required by approved-source policy; ICMR-NIN used as secondary cross-reference.
